1:
The witnesses open the chapter on the glad day the ark came to rest, and it is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who lingers longest over its glory. It was a glorious day, he says, when the ark of God was safely lodged in the tent David had pitched for it; that good man had set his heart upon it and could not sleep contentedly till it was done, the vow he records at Ps. 132:4-5. Henry weighs the ark's new circumstances against its old, and finds them better in every way: it had lain obscure in a country town, in the fields of the wood, and was now removed to the royal city where all might resort to it; it had been neglected like a despised broken vessel, and was now attended with veneration and enquired of; it had borrowed a room in a private house, and now had a habitation of its own. Yet, he adds, they fell short of what the next reign would raise, for this was but a tent, a poor mean dwelling — and here Henry draws his famous paradox: David, who pitched a tent for the ark and continued steadfast to it, did far better than Solomon, who built a temple and yet in his latter end turned his back upon it, so that 'the church's poorest times were its purest.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends instead to the text. He judges that these first three verses are wrongly separated from the close of the preceding chapter, the narrative running still parallel to the account in 2 Sam. 6; where our text says 'God,' Samuel says 'Jehovah,' and Samuel adds that the ark was set 'in its place.' Above all Ellicott notes a deliberate care in the Chronicler: whereas Samuel wrote that 'David offered' the sacrifices, our narrative takes pains to make it clear that the priests and Levites ministered in them — a difference of emphasis, not of fact.

The reformed John Gill passes quickly over these verses as matter already told, referring the reader back to the parallel account; and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies a bird's-eye outline of the whole chapter — David brings the ark into its tent and offers sacrifices, gives portions to the people, appoints ministers before the ark, delivers a solemn thanksgiving, orders the daily service, and sends the people home. Henry gathers the devotional sense the others leave implicit: now that the ark was fixed and near him, David took care that God should have the glory of it by sacrifice and by song, and that the people should have the joy of it. The witnesses do not divide; together they frame the day as one of settled worship, in which a wandering ark comes home and a king turns his gladness into praise.
2:
With the sacrifices ended, David turns from altar to people, and the witnesses read his blessing as the act of a king who is also a father and a prophet. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the wording: the Hebrew has 'the burnt offering' in the singular, as if the many victims were gathered into one great holocaust, and the verse is all but identical with 2 Sam. 6:18, omitting only the divine title 'Sabaoth' at the close — a name, Ellicott suggests, perhaps grown obsolete in the Chronicler's day. That David 'blessed the people in the name of the LORD' he sets beside the priestly benediction of Num. 6:22-27 and the royal blessings of Solomon at 1 Kings 8:14 and 8:55, and the blessing of Moses in Deut. 33:1.

Keil and Delitzsch, the other grammatical-historical voice, look forward from the blessing to the institution it introduces: when the solemnity of the transfer, the sacrificial meal, and the dismissal of the people with a blessing and a distribution of food were all ended, David set in order the service of the Levites in the holy tent on Zion, appointing singers and players to praise and celebrate God as a regular part of worship. They pause on the verb rendered 'to record' — literally 'to bring into remembrance' — and interpret it not as praise in general but by the technical sense it bears in the superscriptions of Ps. 38 and Ps. 70, a liturgical chanting bound to the offering.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the pastoral heart of the blessing. David gave his people what is far better than the royal feast: a blessing in the name of the Lord, as a father and as a prophet, praying to God for them and commending them to his grace. Following the Targum, Henry reads 'in the name of the Word of the Lord' as the essential and eternal Word, who is Jehovah, and through whom all blessings come to us. The witnesses converge on a single portrait: the sacrifices being finished, the king does not dismiss the congregation empty, but sends them away under the benediction of the God whose ark has just come home — the same open-handed generosity that the next verse will show in bread and wine.
3:
The king's bounty to the whole assembly draws the witnesses into a small feast of philology. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes the three gifts at their plain worth: a loaf of bread is a whole cake, and 'a good piece of flesh' he glosses, with the Targum, as 'the sixth part of an ox, and the sixth part of a hin of wine,' referring the reader to the parallel scene in 2 Sam. 6:18-20 and to the old Jewish expositor Jarchi.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses each term more closely. The distribution reaches 'to every man of Israel from man unto woman' — none excluded; the 'loaf' (kikkar) is a round cake, the word used in 1 Sam. 2:36, where the parallel in Samuel has instead a less common term for a sacrificial cake punctured all over, as in Exod. 29:23. The 'good piece of flesh' renders a single rare Hebrew word found only here and in Samuel, which seems to mean a portion — a share, that is, of the victims slain for the peace offerings, since the burnt offerings were wholly consumed on the altar. And the 'flagon of wine,' Ellicott argues, is better read as a raisin-cake, a pressed mass of dried grapes, the very delicacy named in Hosea 3:1 and in the 'raisin-cakes of Kir-hareseth' of Isaiah 16:7.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the gifts from the table to the heart. The people, he says, shall fare the better for this day's solemnity; the king gives them a royal treat in honour of the day, showing himself generous to his subjects as he had found God gracious to him — for those whose hearts are enlarged with holy joy should show it by being open-handed. The witnesses do not contend; the two who count the loaves and the one who reads the giver's heart together render a single picture. The ark's homecoming is no clerical affair sealed off from the crowd: every Israelite, man and woman alike, carries home bread and flesh and sweetness from the king's hand, a household share in the public joy, so that the gladness of the sanctuary overflows into every home in the land.
4:
Here David founds the ministry of song, and the witnesses divide the office into its three named duties. The reformed John Gill states the whole simply: the Levites were appointed to minister before the ark by singing the praises of God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the three verbs that define the charge — 'to remind, and to thank, and to praise Jehovah, the God of Israel' — each a distinct kind of duty in the service of song. 'To record,' he explains, is the technical term for chanting the psalms that accompanied the burning of the Azkarah, the memorial portion of the meal offering presented on the altar in Lev. 2:2, a usage he finds again in the titles of Ps. 38 and Ps. 70. 'To thank' was to perform psalms of invocation and confession of benefits received; 'to praise' was to sing hymns of hallelujah such as the great closing psalms Ps. 146–150. These Levites, he adds, were to minister thus before the ark in the sacred tent on Mount Zion. Ellicott notes too that the verb 'appointed' is literally 'put' or 'placed,' as in Gen. 3:12.

Keil and Delitzsch, the second grammatical-historical witness, set the appointment in its moment: when the solemnity of the transfer and the sacrificial meal and the dismissal were ended, David set in order the service of the Levites in the holy tent on Zion, appointing from among them servants to praise and celebrate God — singers and players to sing psalms as a part of the regular worship. Like Ellicott they linger on 'to record,' reading it by the superscriptions of Ps. 38 and Ps. 70 as a bringing into remembrance, not mere general praise.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the devotional lesson the appointment carries: 'all our rejoicings should express themselves in thanksgivings to him, from whom all our comforts are received.' The witnesses do not divide. They agree that David's great innovation was to fix praise as an ordinance — to set apart men whose whole work was to remember, to thank, and to celebrate before the ark; so that the joy of the day just past would not evaporate, but be gathered up into a standing liturgy of song.
5:
The roll of the singers is called, and the witnesses attend to the order of precedence and the instruments assigned. The reformed John Gill notes a nicety in Asaph's title: he is named 'the chief' of those now appointed to this service, though of the three principal singers of the sanctuary it was Heman who was chief and Asaph who stood next. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke simply refers the reader back to the muster of the Levites in the previous chapter, at 1 Chr. 15:17, where these men were first set in their courses.

Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witnesses, read out the whole company and its scoring: Asaph the chief, and next to him Zechariah, Jeiel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Mattithiah, Eliab, Benaiah, and Obed-edom; Jeiel with psalteries and harps, while Asaph sounded the cymbals, and Benaiah and Jahaziel the priests blew the trumpets continually before the ark. They carry the thought straight on into the next verses, where 'on that day David first' committed to Asaph and his brethren the giving of thanks to the LORD — dwelling on the phrase 'at the beginning' or 'at first,' which they take to mark the very moment when liturgical singing was first introduced into Israel's worship. The 'brethren' of Asaph, they add, are just these Levites named here, appointed with him to the same duty.

The witnesses do not divide, and their small differences only enrich the scene. Gill's reminder that Heman outranked Asaph elsewhere keeps the present precedence in proportion — Asaph leads this choir before the ark on Zion, but the wider hierarchy of the singers stands untouched. Keil and Delitzsch's care over 'at the first' presses the verse's quiet significance: this naming of players and their instruments is not a bare list but the birth-record of temple music, the first ordering of a service of praise that would outlast the tent, the ark's wanderings, and the kingdom itself. Each name, each psaltery and harp and cymbal, is set down because the historian means the reader to see an institution being founded — the ministry of song receiving, on this day, its first appointed ministers.
6:
The two priests with their trumpets close the list, and the witnesses find in this brief verse both a puzzle of names and a hint of design. The reformed John Gill marks only their office: Benaiah and Jahaziel were the priests appointed to blow the trumpets. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott looks closer at both the men and the number. Jahaziel, he observes, is not named in the fuller list of the preceding chapter — unless he be the same as the Eliezer mentioned a little later — so that the priestly trumpeters are not easily matched to the earlier roll. More striking to Ellicott is the arithmetic: counting the singers and players through these verses, the musicians number twelve, a total he takes to answer to the twelve tribes of Israel, the whole nation gathered up and represented in its choir of praise.

On the instruments and their use Ellicott is precise. The 'trumpets' are properly clarions, the straight trumpets of the priests rather than the curved ram's horn; and the word rendered 'continually' is a special Hebrew term, denoting not unbroken noise but service at fixed and regularly recurring times — the appointed hours of worship kept without fail. The whole is done 'before the ark of the covenant of God,' the phrase that fixes the trumpets' place at the very centre of Israel's holy things.

Brief as the verse is, the witnesses let its weight show. What Gill states as a bare duty Ellicott sets within a larger order: the priests blow, but their blowing is timed and stationed, an office as regulated as the singers' song, and the company that performs it is shaped to the measure of all Israel. The trumpet-blasts sounding at their appointed seasons before the ark are, on this reading, no mere flourish but a standing ordinance of the sanctuary. That the priestly clarions and the Levitical strings and cymbals should sound together, each in its proper part, is itself the lesson: praise before the ark is a corporate and ordered thing, priest answering to singer and tribe to tribe, so that the God enthroned above the covenant should never want the praise of his people at the hours he had set.
7:
This verse hands the psalm to Asaph, and around it the witnesses stage the chapter's chief debate: who made this great hymn, and how does it stand to the psalms it so plainly resembles? The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry has no doubt. David, by the Spirit, composed this thanksgiving and delivered it to the chief musician to be sung at the ark's entry into its tent; and though Henry freely grants that it is 'gathered out of several psalms' — the opening from Ps. 105:1 and following down to the twenty-third verse, then the whole of Ps. 96, then a verse from Ps. 136:1, and at the close two verses from Ps. 106 — he takes this as warrant that we too may frame hymns out of David's psalms, joining a part of one to a part of another as devotion requires. The wesleyan-arminian witnesses agree that David is the author. Adam Clarke says plainly that David made the psalm on this occasion and delivered it to Asaph and his brethren to be sung; John Wesley notes that the word 'first' implies David afterward delivered many other psalms into their hands, and observes that we shall find this same song, in the same words, in Ps. 105:1-15 and Ps. 96:1-11, all but the three last verses.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott resists the whole construction. He renders the verse carefully — 'on that day, then, David for the first time gave into the hands of Asaph and his brethren, for giving thanks to Jehovah' — the ode itself standing as the thing given, and allows that the words 'then' and 'on that day' do seem to introduce the psalm, as like phrases introduce the songs of Exod. 15:1 and Judges 5:1. But he marshals four reasons for thinking the ode no original part of the Chronicles, inserted rather by a later hand: first, that it is plainly a cento, portions of three several psalms 'so loosely patched together that the seams are quite visible'; second, that the Psalter itself nowhere ascribes those three psalms to David, which its editors would hardly have left anonymous had they read here a clear claim of Davidic authorship; third, that even here it is not expressly said that David composed the ode, whose ideas and language betray an age later than his; and fourth, that it contains no specific allusion to the occasion it purports to celebrate. If no record survived of the psalms actually sung at the festival, Ellicott reasons, some editor might naturally have supplied the seeming gap from the Psalter.

So the debate is genuinely joined: was this psalm David's own composition, breathed by the Spirit and delivered to Asaph on the day the ark came home, or a later mosaic of familiar psalms strung together and set in David's mouth by an editor supplying what the record lacked? The reformed John Gill, marking only that this was the day the ark was brought to Zion and these ministers appointed, stands with the plain sense the others assume. The witnesses do not resolve the question, but they lay its terms out fully — the same evidence, the composite make of the hymn, read by Henry as David's Spirit-led artistry and by Ellicott as the fingerprint of a later compiler.
8:
The psalm opens with a threefold summons — give thanks, call upon his name, make known his deeds — and the witnesses read the whole ode's architecture out of this first verse. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witnesses, lay out the design: the hymn is a connected and uniform whole, beginning with this call to praise the Lord and seek his face, then exhorting Israel to remember his wondrous works and the covenant he made with the patriarchs to give them Canaan, and confirming the exhortation by recounting how the Lord defended those patriarchs; then summoning all the world to praise him as the only true and almighty God, and all peoples to do him homage; and at last, that his kingdom may be owned among the heathen, calling even inanimate nature to rejoice at his coming to judgment, before it closes with a renewed summons to thankfulness, a prayer for salvation, and a doxology. This single verse, on their reading, is the doorway to that whole movement.

The other grammatical-historical voice, Charles Ellicott, weighs the words. 'Give thanks' is the very verb of the fourth verse, for Ps. 105 is a todah, a thanksgiving, whence its use here; 'call upon his name' is to invoke his help, appealing to him by his revealed name of Jehovah; 'make known' names Israel's own mission to the nations; and 'deeds' is a poetic word for feats, exploits, wonders. Where the summons reaches 'the people,' he notes, the Hebrew means 'the peoples' — the plural that already opens the psalm outward to all the earth.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the devotional force. Let God be glorified in our praises, he urges, and let his honour be the centre in which all the lines meet: we glorify him by our thanksgivings when we give thanks to the Lord, by our prayers when we call on his name, and by our very discourse when we talk of his wondrous works. And the summons is not for ourselves alone; the command to make known his deeds among the people is that others may be edified and instructed, that those who are strangers to him may be led into acquaintance and allegiance and adoration. The witnesses do not divide. They hear in this opening verse both the ground-plan of a great hymn and a personal charge — to thank, to pray, and to publish abroad the works of God.
9:
The summons intensifies from thanks to song, and the witnesses dwell on the piling-up of verbs. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott counts them as three several words for one act of praise: 'sing' unto him, 'sing psalms' unto him — a word that implies a musical accompaniment — and 'talk ye,' or better 'chant ye,' a third term for singing. The object of all three is 'all his wondrous works,' and Ellicott presses the noun: it means his wonders, his miracles, things separate and distinct, and so out of the common run, the sense he finds in the marvels of Exod. 3:20. The verse thus calls for praise that is at once vocal, instrumental, and rehearsed aloud — the whole apparatus of worship bent upon recounting what God alone has done.

Keil and Delitzsch, the second grammatical-historical witness, carry the psalm's opening strophe forward as a single unbroken summons, reading these lines together with those that follow — glory in his holy name, let the heart of them rejoice that seek the LORD, seek the LORD and his strength, seek his face continually — so that this verse is one beat in a sustained call to seek God's presence with gladness. On their arrangement the invitation to sing is inseparable from the invitation to seek; praise and pursuit are one movement of the soul.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry adds the same note from the side of duty: among the ways we are to glorify God, he reckons our songs — 'sing psalms unto him' — and our discourse, for we are to 'talk of all his wondrous works.' The witnesses converge without strain. Praise here is not a single gesture but a whole vocabulary of it: to sing, to make melody with instruments, and to speak of God's wonders to one another, keeping his separate and distinguishing works forever on the tongue. What the first summons of the psalm began, this verse enlarges — the mouth, the harp, and the daily talk of God's people all conscripted into one service of remembrance, so that the wonders which set Israel apart should never fall silent among them.
10:
The summons turns inward, from the voice to the glorying heart, and the witnesses read in it the proper joy of those who seek God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fastens on the phrase 'that seek the LORD,' noting that the verb here is one the Chronicler uses elsewhere for the earnest seeking of God — a term he had employed of Israel's earlier failure to seek the ark at 1 Chr. 13:3, and of the disordered first attempt to move it at 1 Chr. 15:13 — and that this seeking is paired in the very next verse with a second, synonymous word, so that the psalm doubles its call to seek. To glory in his holy name, on this reading, is not to boast in self but to make God's revealed character the whole ground of one's exultation.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the permission hidden in the command. Those who give glory to God's name, he says, are thereby allowed to glory in it — to value themselves upon their relation to God and to venture themselves upon his promise to them; and so he reads the second clause as an invitation to gladness: 'let the heart of those rejoice that seek the Lord,' and much more, he adds, of those that have found him. The joy is not reserved for the arrived but promised to the seeking; the very act of turning Godward is itself a spring of rejoicing.

The two witnesses do not divide, and between them the verse yields a settled lesson. The name of God is holy, and to glory in it is both a duty laid on the worshipper and a dignity granted him — the creature permitted to make his boast in the Creator. And the seeking that Henry commends and Ellicott traces through the Chronicler's own vocabulary is no anxious or joyless labour: the heart that seeks the Lord is bidden to rejoice in the seeking, assured that the God who is sought means to be found. So the psalm's call to praise becomes, in this verse, a call to a particular happiness — the glad, self-forgetful boasting of those whose treasure is the holy name.
11:
The call to seek is now redoubled, and the witnesses hear in it the secret of a strong and steadfast life. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads 'seek the LORD and his strength' against the old confessions of God as the believer's might — the song at the sea that cried 'the LORD is my strength' in Exod. 15:2, and the prophet's 'Jah, Jehovah is a rock of ages' in Isaiah 26:4. To seek 'his face' is to seek his presence, and especially his presence in the sanctuary, where he sets his name to be sought. From this Ellicott draws a compact maxim: 'true devotion is the secret of moral strength' — the strength a man seeks is found only in the seeking of God himself.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse through the very ark whose homecoming the chapter celebrates. To seek the Lord and his strength, he explains, is to seek him 'by the ark of his strength,' the appointed sign in which God manifested himself to his people; and to seek his face continually is to make that seeking no fitful thing but the constant posture of the soul. Henry has already coupled this verse with the one before it, urging that the heart which seeks the Lord is bidden to rejoice; here he adds the note of perseverance — the face of God is to be sought without ceasing.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they lift a single verse into a rule of life. God's strength is not a thing to be had apart from God; it is sought where he is sought, at the ark, in the sanctuary, in his presence. And the seeking is to be 'continual' — not the errand of a festival day but the abiding habit of a people who know that their might is in their God. Ellicott's aphorism gathers up what Henry's exposition displays: those who would be strong must be devout, for the face of the Lord, diligently and unweariedly sought, is itself the fountain of all the strength his servants need. The verse turns the whole summons of the psalm into a discipline of daily nearness to God.
12:
From seeking God the psalm turns to remembering his works, and the witnesses mark the shift into a new strophe of praise. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the summons broadly: the whole psalm, he says, refers to God's wondrous actions among the nations on behalf of Israel, and this verse gathers them under one command — to remember. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that here the second strophe of Ps. 105 begins, and he distinguishes the three objects of remembrance heaped up in the line. The 'marvellous works' are the wonders already named in the ninth verse; 'his wonders' is a different word, meaning his portents — the very signs and prodigies of the New Testament vocabulary; and 'the judgments of his mouth' are his judicial utterances, sentences that execute themselves as soon as spoken, like the word that made light in Gen. 1:3 and the word that smote Egypt in Exod. 12:12.

The two witnesses do not divide, and their emphases dovetail. Clarke fixes the scope of the remembering — it reaches across all God's dealings with the nations for his people's sake — while Ellicott sorts the objects that are to be remembered into their kinds: the marvellous deeds, the portentous signs, and the effectual words of judgment. Together they make the verse a charge against forgetfulness. Israel's whole standing rests on a history of wonders, and that history is to be held in active memory, not left to fade; the miracles that delivered the fathers, the prodigies that overthrew their enemies, and the divine sentences that shaped their course are all to be kept fresh before the mind.

There is a quiet theology in the piling of terms. The God of this psalm does not merely act; he speaks, and his speaking is itself an act, for 'the judgments of his mouth' are not idle threats but words that carry their own fulfilment. To remember them is to reckon with a God whose bare utterance is power — the same God whose word had just brought the ark home, and whose promises, the next verses will insist, are as sure as his judgments are swift. Remembrance, on the witnesses' reading, is the root from which both fear and trust must grow.
13:
The psalm names its hearers, and the witnesses find even in this vocative a small crux of text and a large doctrine of election. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that where our verse reads 'O ye seed of Israel his servant,' the parallel in Ps. 105:6 reads 'seed of Abraham.' The substitution of 'Israel,' he judges, improves the parallelism with 'the children of Jacob' in the next clause and is probably a deliberate correction, though the Syriac and Arabic versions preserve the older 'Abraham.' For 'his servant' the Septuagint reads the plural, 'his servants' — and Ellicott recalls that 'servant of Jehovah' is a standing title of Israel in the prophecies of Isaiah, so that the singular gathers the whole people into one servant-figure before God.

Keil and Delitzsch, the second grammatical-historical witness, take the line up into the running summons of the psalm's first movement, reading 'O ye seed of Israel his servant, ye children of Jacob, his chosen ones' as of a piece with the calls to seek and to remember that surround it. On their arrangement the verse is the hinge that turns the general summons upon its proper audience: it is Israel, and Israel as chosen, who is bidden to remember the wonders and keep the covenant.

The witnesses do not divide, and the weight of the verse lies in its three titles. The hearers are 'the seed of Israel,' heirs of the patriarch's very name; they are 'the children of Jacob,' sprung from the man whom God renamed; and they are 'his chosen ones,' elect not by their own worth but by God's free designation. Whether the text first said Abraham or Israel — and Ellicott lets the question stand — the point is untouched: those summoned to praise are a people set apart, God's own servant and God's own choice. The remembrance and the covenant-keeping the psalm commands are laid, by this verse, upon a chosen people, whose whole obligation to praise flows from the prior fact that God has claimed them for himself. Election is not their boast but their charge; because they are chosen, they must remember.
14:
At the centre of the summons stands a confession, and the witnesses read it as the great paradox of Israel's faith. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states it in a sentence: here is 'the grand thought of Israel that, though Jehovah is their God, he is not theirs exclusively — he governs the wide world.' The two clauses hold that thought in tension. 'He is the LORD our God' claims a covenant nearness, the God of a particular people; 'his judgments are in all the earth' flings the horizon open, for the God so near to Israel is at the same time the ruler of every nation and the whole creation. Israel's privilege, on Ellicott's reading, never shrinks into a possession; the God they call their own is the God of all.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sounds the same two notes as he numbers the ways God is to be glorified in this psalm. He would have us glorify God as the ruler of the whole creation, whose 'judgments are in all the earth,' and then as ours — 'He is the Lord our God' — so that the same verse binds together God's universal government and his particular relation to his people. For Henry the confession is a ground of both awe and confidence: awe, because this near God is the world's great Judge; confidence, because that world-ruling God has bound himself to be 'ours.'

The witnesses do not divide. Between them the verse becomes the pivot of the whole hymn, the point where the praise of a covenant people opens out into the praise of all the earth. Israel is not summoned to worship a tribal deity but the Lord whose sentences run through every land; and yet this Lord of all is not a distant abstraction but the God who has said to Jacob's children, 'I am yours.' The very intimacy of 'our God' is made greater, not smaller, by the universality of 'all the earth': to belong to such a God is to belong to the Maker and Judge of everything. On this confession the psalm will now build its call to the nations, for a God whose judgments fill the earth may rightly be praised by all who dwell in it.
15:
With the covenant the psalm reaches its deepest ground, and the witnesses note a striking change the Chronicler has made in his source. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the parallel strophe in Ps. 105:8 begins not with a command but with a declaration — 'He hath remembered,' that is, 'He will certainly remember' his ancient covenant, so that the exile and oppression of his people can only be a passing thing. Here that assurance is turned into an exhortation, 'Be ye mindful always of his covenant,' the expression modified to suit different circumstances and perhaps shaped by the summons to 'remember' just before it. The 'word which he commanded' Ellicott would render 'the promise which he established' for a thousand generations — a covenant fixed to run the whole length of human history.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the two readings side by side and makes them one lesson. In the psalm it is said that God 'will be ever mindful of his covenant'; here his people are told to 'be mindful' of it — and Henry's inference is exact: 'seeing God never will forget it, we never must.' The covenant is called a thing 'commanded,' he adds, because God has both the authority to make the promise and the ability to make it good, and has bound his people to answer its conditions. Ancient as it is, it is never to be forgotten, for its promises remain sure and pleadable.

The witnesses do not divide; they trade the same truth back and forth. God's remembering grounds our remembering: because he will be ever mindful of what he has sworn, his people are summoned to keep it always before them. And the covenant so remembered is no fragile bargain but a 'word' or 'promise' established for a thousand generations — long outlasting the failures of any single age, so that even exile cannot cancel it. To be mindful of such a covenant is at once a duty and a comfort: a duty, since the God who never forgets calls his people not to forget; a comfort, since the thing to be remembered is a promise as durable as history itself, sure to those who plead it long after the fathers who first received it are gone.
16:
The covenant is now traced to its fountain in the fathers, and the witnesses attend both to the grammar of the promise and to its enduring heirs. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott clears away a phrase: the opening words 'even of the covenant' should be cancelled, he holds, for the object of the sentence is still the 'word' or promise of the previous verse, now specified as the promise God 'made' with Abraham. That verb 'made' is literally 'cut' — the ancient idiom of cutting a covenant, the same phrase that stands in Haggai 2:5 — and the promise to Abraham he refers to the great oath of Gen. 22:16. He notes too a small point of spelling: the name Isaac appears here in its fuller Hebrew form, where the parallel in Ps. 105:9 has the weaker form found also in Amos 7:9.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads this verse together with the two that follow as the naming of the covenant's first partners. It was made, he says, with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, who were long since dead — and yet, he insists, it is 'still sure to the spiritual seed, and the promises of it pleadable.' The covenant does not lapse with the patriarchs' burial; sworn to the fathers, it descends to all who inherit their faith.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott's care over the 'cutting' of the covenant and the oath to Abraham fixes the verse in the solemn history of Gen. 22:16, where God bound himself by his own name; Henry's care is to carry that binding forward to the present, so that a promise cut with Abraham and sworn to Isaac reaches down to the living. Between them the verse becomes a bridge across the generations: the God who summoned Israel to be mindful of his covenant now shows how old and how personal that covenant is — a compact sealed with a named man by a sworn oath, and never since annulled. What was cut with Abraham stands; what was sworn to Isaac holds; and the people now singing before the ark are the very heirs for whose sake the ancient oath was made.
17:
The promise passes to a third generation and is fixed as law, and the witnesses read the verse as the covenant's ratification. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that where our text says God 'hath confirmed' the covenant to Jacob, the sense in the parallel Ps. 105:10 is future — he will confirm it — and that 'the same' which is confirmed is still the 'word' or promise named two verses back, not a new thing but the one ancient pledge now made firm. To confirm it 'for a law,' he explains, is to establish it as a fixed decree, something settled and unalterable; and its second title, 'an everlasting covenant,' sets no term to its duration.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes hold of just that word 'everlasting.' The covenant, he urges, is the great matter of the church's joy and praise precisely because it is everlasting: made with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, who have long since died, it remains sure to their spiritual seed, its promises still pleadable by the living. What the psalm calls a law and an everlasting covenant Henry receives as a standing ground of confidence — a decree that cannot be repealed and a bond that cannot expire.

The witnesses do not divide, and their two emphases complete each other. Ellicott fixes the legal weight of the language: this is no loose understanding but a decree established, a covenant made 'for a law,' as binding on God's part as any statute. Henry fixes its perpetuity: a decree so established is also 'everlasting,' outrunning every generation that receives it. Together they make the verse the keystone of the covenant strophe. What was cut with Abraham and sworn to Isaac is here confirmed to Jacob and to Israel, and confirmed in the strongest terms the language affords — as a fixed law and an eternal covenant. The God who summons his people to remember has himself made his promise unforgettable, graving it as a decree and sealing it as everlasting, so that the singers before the ark rest their whole hope on a word that neither time nor their own unworthiness can dissolve.
18:
The content of the covenant is at last spoken — the gift of a land — and the witnesses attend to the wording of the grant. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes a small roughness in the Hebrew: the rhythm of the line is marred here by the loss of a particle that the parallel in Ps. 105:11 preserves before 'the land of Canaan.' More telling is his gloss on 'the lot of your inheritance': the word rendered 'lot' is literally 'the measuring line,' as in Ps. 16:5, where the psalmist says 'the lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places' — so that Canaan is given not vaguely but as a measured and apportioned domain, a portion surveyed and assigned like an heir's allotted field.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry folds this verse into his reading of the covenant made with the patriarchs. The everlasting covenant of which he has been speaking had this land for its very substance: 'Unto thee will I give the land of Canaan' is the promise confirmed to Jacob and to Israel, sure to the fathers though they died strangers in it, and sure still to their seed. For Henry the naming of Canaan is the concrete pledge behind all the covenant language — the promise was not an abstraction but an inheritance, a real land held out to a real people.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gains from both their angles. Ellicott's 'measuring line' makes the gift precise: Canaan is God's deliberate allotment, portioned out to Israel as a surveyor marks a boundary, not seized but bestowed. Henry's covenant-reading makes the gift gracious: the land comes as the content of an oath, the tangible token of the everlasting covenant. Together they show why the land could anchor a whole people's hope. It was promised by a God who measures out inheritances, and promised within a covenant that cannot lapse; so that even when the fathers wandered as sojourners and their descendants faced exile, the title held. The 'lot of your inheritance' was surveyed by the hand of God himself, and what he has measured and given, the witnesses imply, he does not revoke.
19:
The psalm turns from the promise to the precarious days when it was first given, and the witnesses feel the pathos of the fathers' weakness. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks that the fourth strophe of Ps. 105 opens here, and notes a shift of person: our verse reads 'when ye were but few,' but the psalm and the Septuagint have 'when they were but few,' speaking of the fathers in the third person. The word for 'few' is literally 'men of number' — so few they could be easily counted — a phrase he compares with Jacob's confession of his small and vulnerable house in Gen. 34:30; and 'strangers in it' renders the term for sojourners, resident aliens without title to the land, the very word behind Abraham's plea for a burying-place in Gen. 23:4.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers this verse with those around it into a meditation on God's care for the patriarchs in their unsettled state. When they came as strangers to Canaan and were sojourners in it, he says, when they were few and might easily have been swallowed up, when they were continually on the move and so exposed, and when many bore them ill-will and sought their harm — yet, as the following verses will declare, no man was suffered to do them wrong. Henry reads the smallness and strangeness of the fathers not as a footnote but as the dark ground against which God's protecting hand will shine.

The witnesses do not divide. Between them the verse becomes a portrait of covenant faith at its most exposed: a handful of wandering men, countable on the fingers, holding by promise a land in which they owned not a foot of ground. Ellicott's philology sharpens the weakness — 'men of number,' mere sojourners — while Henry's exposition points forward to the deliverance that answered it. The greatness of the promise and the fewness of its first heirs are set deliberately together, so that the reader may see how much the whole history owed to God alone. Israel's title to Canaan never rested on Israel's strength; it rested, from the first, on the word of the God who gives inheritances to the few and the friendless.
20:
The fathers' wandering is now traced from land to land, and the witnesses read the verse as the record of a homeless pilgrimage kept safe by God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening 'and they went from nation to nation,' and finds in this plainly third-person verb a confirmation of his earlier point: since the psalm here says 'they went,' the 'they' of the previous verse — 'when they were but few' — must be original, and the second-person 'ye' of our text a later adjustment. The reference, he says, is to the wanderings of the patriarchs, who moved from people to people as sojourners; and he notes that the connecting word before 'from one kingdom' is prosaic and is not read in the parallel Ps. 105:13, a small mark of the Chronicler's looser hand.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the verse within his account of the fathers' exposed condition. They were, he says, continually upon the remove, and so continually exposed — driven from place to place, never settled, passing among nations that might easily have destroyed them. This ceaseless motion is, for Henry, part of the danger God so wonderfully overruled: a people always on the road, always among strangers, and yet never abandoned.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott's grammar and Henry's theology meet in a single image of the patriarchs as perpetual travellers. From nation to nation and from kingdom to kingdom they passed, owning no land, resting under no settled protection but God's; and the verse records it not to pity them but to magnify the Keeper who went with them. The wandering that would have swallowed up any ordinary clan became instead the stage on which God displayed his guardianship, for through all those removes — as the next verse will insist — he suffered no man to do them harm. The homelessness of the fathers is thus turned into an argument for praise: a people with no country and no army crossed a hostile world unscathed, because the God who had measured out their inheritance walked with them until the day they should possess it.
21:
Now the psalm reaches the point its wandering has been driving toward — the divine protection over the defenceless fathers — and the witnesses read it as the climax of the strophe. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that this verse was originally the apodosis to the nineteenth: the sense runs, 'when they were but few, and went from nation to nation, he suffered no man to do them wrong.' The clause 'he suffered no man' is, in the Hebrew, 'he permitted to no man,' an idiom he matches with the construction in 2 Sam. 16:11; and he notes that the parallel in Psalms uses a bare accusative and a different word for 'man.' The grammar, for Ellicott, binds the whole picture together: the fewness, the wandering, and the protection are one sentence, one continuous act of God.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fills the verse with its history. Though the fathers were few and exposed, he says, no man was suffered to do them wrong — not the Canaanites, nor the Philistines, nor the Egyptians; and kings were reproved and plagued for their sakes, as Pharaoh was and Abimelech, when they laid hands on the patriarchs or their wives. The God who walked with the wanderers did not merely shield them passively; he went before them into the courts of kings and rebuked the mighty on behalf of the weak.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott's care to reconnect the clauses and Henry's roll of reproved kings together display the same wonder: a scattered, powerless family, moving among empires, and yet untouchable, because an unseen hand turned back every threat and called even monarchs to account for their sakes. The verse is the answer to all the exposure the preceding lines described. However few, however friendless, however far from home, the fathers were never at the mercy of their circumstances; the God of the covenant made their safety his own concern and enforced it upon the greatest powers of the earth. It is this protecting providence — kings rebuked, wrongdoers restrained — that the psalm now sets before Israel as fresh ground for praise, and as a pledge that the God who kept the fathers keeps their seed.
22:
The protection of the fathers is sealed with a divine word — 'Touch not mine anointed' — and here two questions meet the witnesses: who these anointed prophets are, and where this borrowed strophe ends. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes 'mine anointed' of the patriarchs, who held a kind of regal and sacerdotal power in the order of God, and on whose behalf God had so often openly interfered — for Abraham, when Pharaoh's house was plagued in Gen. 12:17 and Abimelech warned in a dream in Gen. 20:3; for Jacob, when Laban was checked in Gen. 31:24 and the men of Shechem overtaken in Gen. 34:26 and the terror of God fell on the cities round about in Gen. 35:5. Yet the title, Clarke allows, may reach further, to all the Jewish people, who were the anointed as being the elect and peculiar people of God — a dignity he connects with the reproach of Christ borne by Moses in Heb. 11:26.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads 'mine anointed' as a plural of the word Messiah, and points out that Abraham and Sarah were promised to be progenitors of kings in Gen. 17:16; the phrase 'do my prophets no harm' he renders more literally 'do no harm against my prophets,' a construction found nowhere else, where the parallel Ps. 105:15 has the usual form. Then Ellicott marks a structural seam: 'we have now reached the first seam in this composite ode.' Where Ps. 105 would go on to the sojourn in Egypt, the Exodus, and the conquest, our hymn breaks off abruptly and makes a fresh start in the next verse with the whole of Ps. 96. The compiler, he says with Delitzsch, 'strung together familiar psalms as a sort of mosaic, to give approximate expression to the festive strains and feelings of the day.'

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the tenderness of the divine warning. The patriarchs were the anointed of the Lord, sanctified by his grace and glory, and his prophets, taught of God and commissioned to teach others; and therefore, Henry says, whoever touches them touches the apple of God's eye, and whoever would harm them does so at his peril. The witnesses do not really divide over the meaning: whether the anointed are the patriarchs alone or all God's elect people, they are precious to God past all violation. And Ellicott's note on the seam, far from unsettling the others, only shows the reader where the psalmist's proof from history ends and the summons to the nations begins — the mosaic turning, at just this verse, from Israel's guarded past to the praise of all the earth.
31:
The praise summoned from Israel now spreads to the whole creation, and the witnesses hear heaven and earth called into the chorus. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports how the Targum expands the opening line: 'Let the supreme angels be glad, and the inhabitants of the earth rejoice.' He notes a curiosity in the paraphrase — the Targumist renders 'the heavens' by the Greek word for angels, angeloi, written out in Hebrew letters — so that on this ancient reading the gladness of 'the heavens' becomes the joy of the angelic host, the highest creatures summoned first to rejoice that the LORD reigns.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes the verse as the voice of God's universal monarchy, to be at once the fear and the joy of all people. Let us reverence that monarchy — 'Fear before him, all the earth' — and let us rejoice in it: 'let the heavens be glad and rejoice, because the Lord reigns,' and because by his providence he establishes the world, so that, though it be moved, it cannot be removed, nor the measures broken which infinite wisdom has taken in its government. For Henry the reign of God is the ground of the creation's stability, and therefore of its gladness: a world upheld by such a King has cause to be glad that he governs it.

The witnesses do not divide. Clarke's Targum lifts the summons to the angels above, and Henry's exposition spreads it through the settled frame of the world below, so that between them the whole scale of creation — the heavenly host, the earth, and the nations that dwell on it — is drawn into one proclamation: 'The LORD reigneth.' The verse widens the psalm's horizon to its utmost. What began as a charge to the seed of Israel to remember and give thanks has become a call for heaven and earth to rejoice together at the kingship of God; and the reason for their joy is the same reason Israel had to praise — that the God who reigns is not a capricious power but the faithful establisher of the world, whose government is as sure as his covenant. Under such a King even the moved and shaken creation stands fast.
34:
As the borrowed psalm of the nations gives way to its close, the witnesses recognise a familiar liturgical cadence. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that this verse — 'O give thanks unto the LORD, for he is good, for his mercy endureth for ever' — is the beautiful formula with which several of the later psalms begin, such as Ps. 106, Ps. 107, Ps. 118, and Ps. 136, and which sounds again in the promise of restored praise in Jer. 33:11. Its function here, he observes, is to round the ode off: the hymn 'concludes with the thought from which it started,' for it opened, back at the eighth verse, with the very same summons to give thanks. The whole composite psalm is thus framed between two calls to thanksgiving, an inclusion that binds its patched-together parts into one.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already marked this verse as drawn from the great antiphon of Ps. 136:1, whose refrain 'for his mercy endureth for ever' he elsewhere makes the standing burden of the sanctuary's song. For Henry the endless mercy of God is the fittest matter of endless praise: a goodness that never fails deserves a thanksgiving that never stops. Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the architecture of the whole ode, place this verse at its proper hinge — after the summons to the nations and the call to rejoicing creation, the hymn returns to its keynote of thankfulness before its final prayer and doxology.

The witnesses do not divide. Between Ellicott's formula and Henry's refrain the verse stands as the psalm's turn homeward: having ranged from Israel's covenant history to the praise of all the earth and the gladness of the heavens, the song comes back to rest on the simplest and surest of confessions — that the LORD is good, and that his mercy has no end. It is a mercy that reaches back to the fathers and forward past every exile, and precisely because it 'endureth for ever' it can gather up the whole sweep of the hymn in a single line. The thanksgiving with which the ode began is the thanksgiving in which it comes to rest.
35:
Amid the praise the psalm breaks into petition — 'Save us, gather us, deliver us from the heathen' — and the witnesses ask how such a prayer for deliverance came to stand in a hymn of thanksgiving. The reformed John Gill takes the words as a direction to the singers to voice this plea and the doxology that follows, and reads them prophetically: David, he says, penned them 'by a spirit of prophecy, foreseeing the people of Israel would be in captivity among the heathen,' scattered and oppressed and needing to be gathered home. Yet Gill honestly records another opinion — that 'some think these were added by Ezra,' since even in Ezra's day, though a return from captivity had come, many still remained dispersed. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the verse the same prophetic way: the psalm was made by David for the use of the church not only in that present time but in future ages, in which he foresaw by the spirit of prophecy that the Israelites would forsake God and for their apostasy be dispersed among the heathen. And Wesley draws the pastoral rule: 'in the midst of our praises we must not forget to pray for those servants of God that are in distress' — for we are members one of another, and their salvation is our own.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke dwells on the name invoked, 'O God of our salvation.' Because he is the saving God, Clarke says, we may boldly pray to him to save us; and to address God under the very attribute whose help we need is a great inspirer of confidence. He turns it into a little litany of the needy soul: 'I am weak; Almighty God, help me! I am ignorant; O thou Father of lights, teach me! I am lost; O merciful God, save me!'

The witnesses divide only over the verse's origin. Was it David, seeing by prophecy the captivity to come, who set this cry for gathering into his festal song, or a later hand — Ezra, some suppose — who added it when the dispersion was a present grief? Gill lays both views side by side, inclining to the prophetic David but recording the alternative; Wesley holds firmly to David's foresight. On the verse's use they are wholly one: whoever first framed it, it teaches the church at the height of her joy to remember her scattered and suffering members, and to make even her thanksgiving carry a prayer — 'save us, gather us' — for all the people of God still far from home.
37:
The festival over, the chapter settles into a standing order of worship, and the witnesses read this verse as the founding of daily praise before the ark. The reformed John Gill notes simply that David left Asaph and his brethren, whom he had appointed, for the continual service of the ark. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witnesses, render the charge 'to minister according to the matter of each day on its day' — the service proper to every day performed without fail — and pause over a textual snarl in the following clause: 'Obed-edom and their brethren' cannot be right as it stands, for the plural 'their' shows that at least one name has dropped out before it, and the Obed-edom here called the son of Jeduthun sits awkwardly beside the singer and the doorkeeper of the same name elsewhere in the chapter.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes this verse the text for a meditation on constancy in worship. The service of God, he says, is not only the work of a solemn day now and then, brought in to grace a triumph, but ought to be the work of every day; and so David here settles it, putting worship into a method his ministers must keep in their several posts. Henry marvels at the arrangement the age required: ever since Eli's time the ark and the altar had been parted, and they remained so till the temple was built, the ark now on Zion and the altar at Gibeon. He cannot fully explain why David, so zealous for the law, did not reunite them — perhaps the old curtains of Moses' tabernacle were too worn to shelter the ark, and he would not make all new when the temple was so near — but he took care that neither should be neglected. At Jerusalem, before the ark, Asaph and his brethren were to minister continually with songs of praise. And here Henry notes a thing of great weight: no sacrifices were offered there, nor incense burnt, because the altars were not there; yet David's prayers were directed as incense, and the lifting up of his hands as the evening sacrifice, in the words of Ps. 141:2 — 'so early,' he says, 'did spiritual worship take place of ceremonial.'

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same in brief: David 'appointed them their work and station there'; no incense was burnt nor sacrifice offered, the altars being elsewhere, but David's prayers were directed as incense and the lifting of his hands as an evening sacrifice, so early did spiritual worship take place of ceremonial. The witnesses do not divide. What Gill states as a bare appointment, Henry and Wesley lift into a principle: before the ark, with no altar and no victim, a ministry of pure praise and prayer was set up to run every day — a worship of the lips and the lifted heart that already, under the old economy, pointed beyond sacrifice to the spiritual service God most desires.
38:
The roster of the ark's servants continues with the doorkeepers, and the witnesses attend to the numbers and the offices. The reformed John Gill notes that these Levites, the brethren of Asaph and of Obed-edom, were left before the ark to minister there. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witnesses, read the fuller detail: Obed-edom with their brethren, sixty-eight in number, and Obed-edom the son of Jeduthun together with Hosah appointed to be porters — the keepers of the doors of the sacred tent. They carry the sentence forward to the parallel appointment at Gibeon, where Zadok the priest and his brethren were left before the tabernacle of the LORD at the high place, the Bamah, that was there.

Here Keil and Delitzsch raise a thoughtful question. It is surprising, they say, that no priest is named as overseer of the sacrificial worship at the tent of the ark, as Zadok is named over the sanctuary at Gibeon. But the omission is accounted for by the design of the chapter, which properly concerns only the ordering of the sacred music; Zadok is brought in over Gibeon merely to introduce the singers and players assigned to that place. Without doubt, they add, it was Abiathar as high priest who had the oversight of the worship at the sanctuary of the ark on Zion, though the historian, intent on the music, does not pause to say so.

The witnesses do not divide. Between Gill's plain notice and Keil and Delitzsch's closer reading, the verse fills out the picture of a fully staffed sanctuary: singers to praise, players to accompany, and now porters to keep the doors, sixty-eight brethren in all, with Obed-edom and Hosah at their head. The care lavished on the doorkeepers is itself instructive — the God whose ark had once been captured and carried off is now surrounded with an ordered household of ministers, some to lift the song and some to guard the threshold, so that nothing about his worship is left to chance. And the very gap the witnesses notice — the unnamed overseer of the ark's worship — reminds the reader that the chapter's whole eye is on the music, the ministry of praise for whose sake all these names are set down.
39:
With Zadok stationed at Gibeon the chapter completes its double arrangement of worship, and the witnesses explain the strange spectacle of two sanctuaries at once. The reformed John Gill notes only that David left Zadok and his fellow-priests, having appointed them to their charge. The wesleyan-arminian witnesses supply the reason for the division. John Wesley identifies Zadok as the chief priest at Gibeon, where the tabernacle and altar made by Moses still stood and where the ordinary sacrifices were offered and the stated worship of God performed, while the extraordinary worship before the ark — resorted to on great occasions, when God was to be consulted through the high priest — was carried on at Jerusalem under Abiathar.

Adam Clarke lays the whole arrangement out plainly: both Zadok and Abiathar were high priests at this time; David established Zadok at Gibeon, where the ark had rested through all the days of Saul, and Abiathar at Jerusalem, where the ark now was. So there were, for a season, two high priests and two distinct services — yet, Clarke is careful to add, only one ark. How long the service at Gibeon was kept up he cannot say, but the principal functions were doubtless performed at Jerusalem, where the ark of the covenant stood.

The witnesses do not divide. Between Wesley's account of the two kinds of worship and Clarke's of the two high priests, the verse resolves into a coherent picture of a transitional order: the Mosaic altar and its daily sacrifices continuing at Gibeon under Zadok, and the ark with its new ministry of praise and its oracle established on Zion under Abiathar, until the temple should gather both together again. It was an irregular season, two centres and two priesthoods where the law envisioned one; but David, unable for the present to reunite ark and altar, would let neither be neglected, and so maintained both — the ceremonial worship in its ancient place and the spiritual worship in its new one, each kept up in full until the day when Solomon's temple should make them one. Clarke's caution that there was but one ark, though two seats of service, keeps the arrangement from any hint of a divided religion: the covenant sign remained single, and to it the great approaches of the nation were still made, even while the daily altar smoked on at Gibeon.
40:
At Gibeon the ancient sacrifices go on, and the witnesses read the verse as the charter of the altar's continual service. The reformed John Gill marks that to offer burnt offerings was the work of the priests only, the duty proper to Zadok and his brethren there. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witnesses, unfold the standing order: the priests were to offer burnt offerings upon the altar continually, morning and evening, and to do according to all that is written in the law of the LORD which he commanded Israel — the whole Mosaic ritual kept up without abatement. They add that with these priests were joined Heman and Jeduthun, the other two chief musicians, and the rest of the chosen singers expressly named, appointed like their brethren on Zion to give thanks because the LORD's mercy endures for ever.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the reason these ceremonies were not allowed to lapse. The sacrifices commanded to Israel, he says, must be kept up, because, however in their own nature they were inferior to prayer and praise, yet as they were types of the mediation of Christ their observance was of mighty importance. The altar at Gibeon was not a rival to the praise on Zion but its necessary complement, pointing forward, through every morning and evening offering, to the one true sacrifice it prefigured.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill fixes the office, Keil and Delitzsch the daily round and the singers joined to it, and Wesley the deeper meaning that made the round worth keeping. Between them the verse shows David maintaining the full weight of the law even while he pioneers the new worship of song: burnt offerings morning and evening, everything written in the law performed, and thanksgiving raised over it all. The ceremonial and the spiritual go forward together, morning sacrifice and unending praise, the shadow and the substance side by side — the altar bearing its continual witness to the coming Mediator while the singers celebrate the mercy that endures for ever. Neither is neglected, and each interprets the other. What the singers proclaim in words — the mercy that endures for ever — the priests enact in the daily offering, so that voice and altar together keep the whole worship of Gibeon reaching beyond itself to the God it serves.
41:
The singers at Gibeon are now named beside the priests, and the witnesses dwell on the refrain that was the burden of their song. The reformed John Gill notes that the 'with them' of the verse means with Zadok and the priests who ministered at the altar in Gibeon — the musicians set alongside the sacrificers. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witnesses, identify the singers: with the priests were Heman and Jeduthun, the two other chief musicians, together with the rest of the chosen who were 'expressed by name,' the famous singers marked out for this service, among them those of the earlier lists who had not been assigned to Asaph on Zion, and doubtless others whose names have not come down to us. All were appointed to one end — to give thanks to the LORD, because his mercy endures for ever.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fastens on that closing clause as the very theme of the Gibeon choir. At the altars, he says, David appointed singers to give thanks to the Lord, and the burden of all their songs must be, 'For his mercy endureth for ever.' For Henry this endless mercy is the fittest matter of a ceaseless praise: a service that went on morning and evening, day after day, needed a theme as inexhaustible as itself, and found it in the mercy that never ends.

The witnesses do not divide. Between Gill's placing of the singers, Keil and Delitzsch's naming of them, and Henry's seizing of their refrain, the verse rounds out the worship at Gibeon to match the worship on Zion: not sacrifice alone, but sacrifice crowned with song. The same note struck before the ark is struck again before the altar — thanksgiving for a mercy that endures for ever — so that in both places, and by both companies of ministers, one confession rises continually. David's whole design appears in miniature here: wherever God is served in Israel, whether by the offering of victims or the lifting of the voice, the enduring mercy of the LORD is to be owned and celebrated without end.
42:
The instruments of the Gibeon service are named, and around the phrase 'musical instruments of God' the witnesses gather, one of them into open controversy. The reformed John Gill first settles the grammar: the words mean that with Heman and Jeduthun were the trumpets and cymbals — or, as Kimchi takes it, that these instruments were with them; or, as Piscator supplies the sense, that David 'left' the trumpets and cymbals with them for the service. Keil and Delitzsch, the grammatical-historical witnesses, read 'Heman and Jeduthun with trumpets and cymbals for those that should make a sound, and with the instruments of God's song,' and note that the sons of Jeduthun were the porters — the same distribution of song and doorkeeping seen on Zion.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads 'instruments of God' as instruments appropriated to the worship of God, not such as were used on other occasions, and draws his usual careful line: 'between common mirth and holy joy there is a vast difference, and the limits and distances between them must be carefully kept up.' But it is Adam Clarke, the other wesleyan-arminian voice, who turns the verse into a battleground. He surveys the versions — the Vulgate's 'to sing to God,' the Septuagint's 'of the sons of God,' and above all the Syriac, which he quotes at length: that these were 'upright men who did not sing unto God with instruments of music, nor with drums, nor with pipes, nor with cymbals, but sang before the Lord Almighty with a joyous mouth, and with a pure and holy prayer, and with innocence and integrity.' Seizing on this, Clarke launches a long series of pointed questions against instrumental music in Christian worship: did God ever ordain instruments of music in his worship? can they be used in Christian assemblies according to the spirit of Christianity? did Christ or his apostles ever command or sanction them, or were they ever used in the apostolic Church? He doubts that mere sounds, however melodious, in which no word nor sentiment is uttered, can be reckoned praise to God, and judges the argument from their use in the Jewish service 'futile in the extreme when applied to Christianity.'

Here the witnesses genuinely part company, though only Clarke presses the point. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch take the instruments as a plain fact of the service, Wesley guards their sacred appropriation without questioning their use, and Clarke alone would banish them from Christian worship altogether. Keil and Delitzsch close the chapter's business by noting the last verse, where all the people departed to their homes and David 'returned to bless his house' — not merely to greet it but to pronounce a blessing on it, as he had blessed the people earlier in the day. The verse thus leaves the reader with a fully instrumented sanctuary and a lone dissenting voice, faithfully recorded: the trumpets and cymbals sounding before the LORD, and Clarke's protest that under the gospel the purest praise may be the praise of the lips alone.
43:
The chapter ends as the assembly disperses, and the witnesses read the quiet close with an eye to what the Chronicler has kept and what he has passed over. The reformed John Gill notes simply that all the people departed, every man to his house, having accompanied the ark to its place, praised the Lord for it, and been refreshed with the food the king had dealt out. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that this verse is a duplicate of the close of the parallel scene in 2 Sam. 6:19-20; 'departed' is here plural where Samuel has the singular, and 'returned' is more exactly 'went round,' a word very like the Samuel reading and easily confused with it.

The point on which Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch alike dwell is the Chronicler's omission. In 2 Sam. 6:20 and following, David's return to bless his house is met by Michal's scornful reproach and the bitter exchange that ensues; but the Chronicler, both witnesses agree, has left the whole scene out as a matter of purely domestic interest, out of place in a history so wholly concerned with the sacred institutions. Keil and Delitzsch add that the earlier reference to Michal's contempt, in the previous chapter, seemed to the historian enough; and they insist that 'to bless his house' means just that — not to greet his household but to pronounce a blessing upon it, exactly as David had blessed the people in the name of the Lord earlier in the day.

The witnesses do not divide. Between Gill's contented dispersing of the people and the two grammarians' notice of the silence over Michal, the verse gathers up the whole chapter's temper. The day of the ark's homecoming ends not in strife but in benediction: the people go home satisfied, and the king turns from blessing the nation to blessing his own household, carrying the worship of the sanctuary across his own threshold. What the parallel in Samuel darkens with a family quarrel, the Chronicler leaves in unbroken light, keeping his gaze fixed where his whole book is fixed — on the ordered worship of God, and on a king who, having settled the ark and its praise, goes home to make his house, too, a place of blessing.
